seo.title: Moving to the Netherlands from Germany | Registration, Documents, Costs & Practical Setup
seo.description: Planning a move from Germany to the Netherlands? Explore EU move rules, registration, BSN, document requirements, housing, banking, and the practical steps to settle in.
keywords: moving to the netherlands from germany
living in the netherlands as a german

faqOverrides.q: Do Germans need a visa to live in the Netherlands?
faqOverrides.a: German citizens are EU citizens. For ordinary residence based on EU free movement, you generally do not need a visa or residence permit in the same way as non-EU nationals. You must still meet the conditions that apply to your situation. Confirm details on the IND page for EU, EEA, and Swiss citizens and on Government.nl.

faqOverrides.q: Do Germans need an MVV to move to the Netherlands?
faqOverrides.a: An MVV (provisional residence permit as an entry visa) is part of many non-EU long-stay procedures. It is not the standard framework for Germans exercising EU free movement for ordinary residence. If your situation is unusual, check official sources or seek tailored advice.

faqOverrides.q: How do I register after moving from Germany?
faqOverrides.a: If you will live in the Netherlands for more than four months, you normally register with your municipality. You must register within five working days of arrival once you have a residential address. The municipality issues your BSN. See our municipality registration guide and official Government.nl information.

faqOverrides.q: Do German birth or marriage certificates need legalisation for the Netherlands?
faqOverrides.a: Netherlands Worldwide states that many German civil-status documents—including birth, marriage, divorce, and death certificates—do not have to be legalised for use in the Netherlands, and documents in German often do not need translation. Other document types or uses may differ; always verify for your specific document and authority.

faqOverrides.q: What should I prioritise in the first weeks?
faqOverrides.a: Most people focus on municipal registration and BSN, Dutch basic health insurance where required, a bank account, and DigiD. Housing search intensity is often the hardest part, not immigration paperwork.

faqOverrides.q: When might immigration lawyers or visa consultants still help?
faqOverrides.a: Complex family situations, mixed-nationality households, or specialised legal questions may warrant professional advice. For typical EU moves, many people rely on gemeente guidance and official web sources first.

customNotes: EU cross-border movers often prioritise housing platforms and banking after registration planning.
